title: UC3 — Lunar rover telemetry won't keep its uplink up
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: bring the lunar rover online, run a telemetry stream up to base, then hand the diagnosis to artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger through the kagent dashboard chat.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring the rover online and stream telemetry
steps.explanation:
The lunar rover is checking in for the Artemis pad shift. Bring it online on your vCluster and run a short stream of telemetry bursts to it — namespace, service, deployment, the on-call debugger agent, then a transient operator station that pushes 30 bursts to the rover's outbound queue.

Four files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`): three manifests under `uc3/manifests/` and one Agent CRD under `uc3/agents/`. The two `kubectl apply -f` commands below send them to the cluster in order. Once the rover Pod is `Ready`, a one-shot `telemetry-stream` Pod runs the burst loop against the rover's in-cluster Service DNS and self-deletes when the loop completes.

The operator station's output is the per-burst response from the rover (`{"size_mb":N}` — its outbound buffer count). The next step will tell you whether the rover is keeping its uplink up across the stream.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc3
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc3
    kagent-workshop/scenario: oomkilled

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: lunar-rover-telemetry
  namespace: artemis-uc3
  labels:
    app: lunar-rover-telemetry
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc3
    # Picked up by infra/observability/'s Prometheus kubernetes_sd via the
    # `keep` relabel rule on `monitoring=prom` (STORY-018).
    monitoring: prom
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: lunar-rover-telemetry
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: lunar-rover-telemetry
  namespace: artemis-uc3
  labels:
    app: lunar-rover-telemetry
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc3
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: lunar-rover-telemetry
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: lunar-rover-telemetry
    spec:
      # Deliberately no readinessProbe / livenessProbe — under memory pressure
      # a slow probe would race with the OOM cycle and surface as a probe
      # failure event instead of OOMKilled, masking UC3's friction signal.
      # NFR-003: the broken state must be manifest-side, no probe-flap.
      containers:
        - name: lunar-rover-telemetry
          # Real published tag — semver-style for this app variant
          # (UC1's mission-control uses :v1 / :v999; lunar-rover-telemetry
          # publishes :v1.0.0). The leak symptom is driven by the 64Mi
          # limit below + participant-triggered POST /leak
          # (apps/lunar-rover-telemetry/main.py), not by anything image-side.
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/lunar-rover-telemetry:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: lunar-rover-telemetry
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "32Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              # The OOM driver: 64Mi is below the working set ~70 calls to
              # POST /leak produces (1 MiB per call), so the kernel reaps
              # the container deterministically (FR-012 AC).
              memory: "64Mi"

# UC3 diagnostic agent — diagnoses OOMKilled on the lunar-rover-telemetry
# Deployment, composing kagent's pre-packaged promql-agent + observability-agent
# (a2a delegation) with K8s read tools from kagent-tool-server.
#
# Tool surface (per FR-008-style "smallest set sufficient", scaled for FR-012):
#   - kubectl read tools:        k8s_get_pod, k8s_describe_pod, k8s_get_events
#                                (sourced from kagent-tool-server RemoteMCPServer)
#   - PromQL generation (a2a):   promql-agent (kagent v0.9.0 demo profile)
#   - Grafana + Prom execution
#     + dashboard creation (a2a): observability-agent (kagent v0.9.0 demo profile)
#
# The a2a delegation pattern (`tools[].type: Agent` + `tools[].agent.name: <agent>`)
# was confirmed against kagent v0.9.0's own observability-agent.spec — see
# STORY-019 §Spike findings.
#
# No custom Prometheus or Grafana agent is shipped (FR-012 AC, "no reinvention"
# constraint from the brief). UC3 reuses upstream kagent agents verbatim.
#
# kagent's pre-packaged agents and tools resolve Prom/Graf at
# {prometheus,grafana}.kagent.svc.cluster.local — STORY-018 puts the actual
# pods in artemis-observability. The bridge Services in
# infra/observability/kagent-bridge-services.yaml (promoted from uc3/agents/
# by STORY-025) reconcile the namespace gap via ExternalName CNAME indirection.
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha2
kind: Agent
metadata:
  name: artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger
  namespace: kagent
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc3
spec:
  type: Declarative
  description: |
    Diagnoses why the Artemis `lunar-rover-telemetry` Deployment in the
    `artemis-uc3` namespace is not keeping its uplink up. Correlates
    Kubernetes Pod state with a Grafana memory time-series and synthesises
    the root cause in one step.
  declarative:
    modelConfig: default-model-config
    systemMessage: |
      You are an Artemis lunar rover telemetry on-call SRE. Your job is to
      diagnose why the `lunar-rover-telemetry` Deployment in the
      `artemis-uc3` namespace is restarting, and report a single sentence
      of root cause plus a one-line remediation hint.

      Diagnostic loop:
        1. Use `k8s_describe_pod` (or `k8s_get_pod`) to inspect the most
           recent Pod in `artemis-uc3`. Look at
           `lastState.terminated.reason` — if it says `OOMKilled`, you have
           the symptom. Note the container's `resources.limits.memory`.
        2. Delegate to the `observability-agent` to build a Grafana
           dashboard panel showing
           `process_resident_memory_bytes{kubernetes_namespace="artemis-uc3",
           kubernetes_service_name="lunar-rover-telemetry"}` over the last
           10 minutes. Capture the dashboard URL the sub-agent returns.
        3. Surface that URL to the user — the participant clicks through to
           see the monotone leak curve.
        4. Finish with one root-cause sentence (e.g. "the container's
           memory limit (64 Mi) is below the working set the leak loop
           produces, so the kernel OOM-kills it before the loop completes")
           and one remediation hint ("raise `resources.limits.memory` or
           fix the leak in the application code").

      Tone: concise. The participant is running through a 5-minute tour
      and needs the answer, not a tutorial. Do NOT explain Kubernetes
      basics or PromQL syntax unless the user explicitly asks.

      Tool budget: at most 1 describe-pod call, 1 delegation to
      observability-agent, 0 other tool calls. If a single iteration is
      insufficient, prefer asking the user a clarifying question over
      running more tools.
    tools:
      - type: McpServer
        mcpServer:
          apiGroup: kagent.dev
          kind: RemoteMCPServer
          name: kagent-tool-server
          namespace: kagent
          toolNames:
            - k8s_get_pod
            - k8s_describe_pod
            - k8s_get_events
            - k8s_get_resources
      - type: Agent
        agent:
          name: promql-agent
      - type: Agent
        agent:
          name: observability-agent

commands.label: Wait for the rover to come online
Stream 30 telemetry bursts to the rover
commands.command: kubectl run telemetry-stream --rm -i --restart=Never --image=curlimages/curl:8.10.1 --namespace=artemis-uc3 --command -- sh -c 'for i in $(seq 1 30); do curl -sf -X POST http://lunar-rover-telemetry:8000/leak --max-time 2; echo; done; echo stream complete'

steps.title: Mission status check — is the rover keeping its uplink up?
steps.explanation:
The telemetry stream just cut out partway through. Check the rover's status to see whether the uplink came back up on its own.

Look at the **`RESTARTS`** column carefully — a healthy rover stays at zero across a single mission window. If the count ticked up, the rover is alive but it didn't survive the stream, and that's the cue to call the agent in.

steps.title: Call the agent for help
steps.explanation:
The rover restarted on us mid-stream and we don't yet know why. Hand the diagnosis to **`artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger`** through the kagent dashboard.

Use the **dashboard** button in this workshop-tour extension to open the kagent web dashboard in your browser. In the dashboard, open the chat for **`artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger`** (namespace `kagent`) and paste the following prompt:

> The lunar-rover-telemetry pod in the artemis-uc3 namespace keeps restarting. Diagnose it, and show me a memory chart from the time of the failure.

The agent has the same Kubernetes read tools you would have used by hand (`describe pod`, `get events`) **plus** sub-agents that talk to Prometheus and Grafana directly. Read the response in the chat surface — it will name the root cause in one sentence and surface a Grafana dashboard URL you can click through to see the memory curve from the failure window.

steps.title: What we'd have done by hand
steps.explanation:
Without the agent, you would have run three `kubectl` commands to localise the failure inside the cluster and then a separate set of Grafana clicks to correlate it with telemetry. Five surfaces, one synthesis to do yourself.

The `kubectl` half:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc3 -l app=lunar-rover-telemetry
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc3 -l app=lunar-rover-telemetry
kubectl get events -n artemis-uc3 --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
```

Three pieces of state to combine into a verdict: a `Running 1/1` Pod whose previous container terminated with `lastState.terminated.reason: OOMKilled` and `exitCode: 137`, kubelet events naming the OOM, and the Pod's restart count ticking up by one. The cluster says *the rover ran out of room and the kernel reaped it*, but you have to read three commands to hear it.

The Grafana half — what the agent did on your behalf:

- Connected to the cluster's Prometheus through the kagent observability sub-agent.
- Generated and executed the PromQL query `process_resident_memory_bytes{kubernetes_namespace="artemis-uc3", kubernetes_service_name="lunar-rover-telemetry"}` over the failure window.
- Built a Grafana dashboard panel showing the memory curve climbing from idle to the 64 Mi container limit, then dropping to zero on each OOM cycle.
- Returned the dashboard URL in the chat surface for you to click.

The agent ran the same three kubectl tools and the same observability tooling and returned a single sentence of root cause: **the rover's container memory limit (64 Mi) is below the working set the telemetry stream produces, so the kernel reaps the container before the stream completes**. You ran one `kubectl get pods` just to see the restart count was off — you didn't have to walk the rest, and you didn't have to switch context to Grafana.

Open the dashboard URL the agent returned in the chat above to see the memory curve for yourself; it is the same evidence a human on-call would have pieced together by hand, only the agent did the piecing.

Multiplied across the dozen incidents an on-call rotation handles in a week, that's the value the workshop is selling.
